title: Phase-Topology Classification of Memristor Hysteresis Loops via Self-Crossings
authors: Ovidiu-Zeno Lipan, Eric Neuhaus, Rafael Schio Wengenroth Silva, Soumen Pradhan, Fabian Hartmann, Leonardo K. Castelano, Ana Luiza Costa Silva, Sven Höfling, Victor Lopez-Richard
abstract: Memristive devices have revolutionized non-volatile memory and neuromorphic computing, yet the geometry of their hysteresis loops -- in particular, the occurrence and robustness of multiple self-crossings -- remains poorly understood. Here we introduce a topological and algebraic framework that treats the number of transverse self-intersections of a memristor hysteresis loop as a robust integer-valued invariant. Drawing on differential topology, singularity theory, and cusp catastrophe, we employ discriminants and resultants to stratify the six-dimensional parameter space. This approach partitions the parameter space into structurally stable regions separated by explicitly computable catastrophe surfaces. We demonstrate that the crossing number remains strictly invariant under continuous deformations and changes only at self-tangencies or cusp singularities, thereby providing a complete classification of all multi-lobed hysteresis behaviors. These insights bridge device physics with modern singularity theory and suggest a clear roadmap for exploiting higher-order memory effects in next-generation electronics and brain-inspired hardware.
summary: This paper provides a rigorous mathematical framework to classify the different shapes of memristor hysteresis loops based on their topological self-crossings. By using catastrophe theory, it predicts how changing voltage amplitude or frequency can cause sudden changes in the device's memory characteristics. This is significant for designing more complex, multi-lobed memristive devices for neuromorphic computing.
main_problem: The geometric complexity and topological classification of self-crossings in memristor hysteresis loops are poorly understood, specifically regarding how parameter changes drive transitions between different loop morphologies.
main_result: The authors establish a topological framework where the number of self-crossings is a robust integer-valued invariant, using singularity theory to map out the parameter space boundaries (catastrophe surfaces) where these crossings emerge or disappear.
method: The study employs differential topology, singularity theory (specifically the cusp catastrophe), and algebraic tools like discriminants and resultants to stratify a six-dimensional parameter space.
model_or_system: A generalized voltage-controlled memristive system where memductance is a perturbed constant admittance modulated by an internal state variable that evolves via a relaxation-type equation.
key_observables: Crossing number (number of self-intersections), susceptance polynomial roots, and local admittances.
important_parameters: Dimensionless frequency (p = omega*tau), drive amplitude (A), and polynomial coefficients (xi_k) defining the nonlinear response.
main_assumptions: The state evolution is simplified to a linear relaxation-type equation, and the memductance is assumed to be a linear perturbation of a constant admittance.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the mapping of hysteresis loops to a unit circle, the identification of bifurcation boundaries in parameter space, and the visualization of cusp catastrophe dynamics and phase-space trajectories.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of loop geometry, introduces a generalized memristive model, applies singularity theory to classify parameter regions, uses algebraic tools to find bifurcation boundaries, and concludes with a discussion on the implications for hardware design.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Mapping of the current–voltage hysteresis loop from the unit circle. (a) Unit circle in the normalized voltage–derivative plane. The horizontal axis shows the normalized voltage x = V/A, while the vertical axis shows its time derivative y. As time progresses over one full period, the point (x(t), y(t)) traces the entire circle exactly once. (b) Resulting current–voltage hysteresis loop I(x, y). Each point on the circle in (a) is mapped to a point on the loop in (b) at the same instant in time. The upper semicircle (red) maps to the ascending (positive) branch of the hysteresis loop, while the lower semicircle (blue) maps to the descending (negative) branch. Arrows indicate the...

FIG. 3: Relationship between the susceptance B(x) (black) and the self-crossings of the current–voltage hysteresis loop (red and blue, same as in Fig. 1). The thin vertical segments indicate the roots of B(x), which mark the voltages at which the hysteresis loop crosses itself (besides the crossing at zero-voltage).

FIG. 2: Constraints for hysteresis loops confined to quadrants I and III in the I-V plane. The passivity constraint (18) is satisfied in the colored regions and violated in the white regions. Both panels are generated using τ = 1 and the same generating function as in Fig. 1.

Fig.2 (a) underscores the importance of carefully as- sessing the generating function model, as there is no as- surance that a model valid for one amplitude will work for all smaller values. For example, an amplitude of 0.2 proves unsuitable, as the loop extends into quadrant IV for positive input voltage, violating the condition of con- finement to quadrant I. This issue persists within a range of amplitudes near 0.2. Furthermore, amplitudes above 0.79 result in the loop extending into quadrant II for large negative voltages, breaking the confinement to quadrant III.
